agent1_snippets.content: Pantothenate deficiency can play a vital role in acetylcholine deficiency, neurodegeneration, myelin loss, and age-related dementias like Huntington disease. [11] Nevertheless, more studies are still needed to test the clinical efficacy of vitamin B5 in these conditions.

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Dyslipidemia is another field that can potentially benefit from vitamin B5 use. Since vitamin B5 is essential for the biosynthesis of coenzyme A, it plays a role in the metabolism of lipids. One study concluded that pantethine (a derivative of vitamin B5) lowered cardiovascular disease risk markers in low to moderate-risk participants. [10] These risk markers include LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Fundamentals -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
agent1_snippets.content: Pantothenic acid, or vitamin B5, is an essential water-soluble vitamin that acts as a key component of coenzyme A and phosphopantetheine, which are crucial to fatty acid metabolism. Pantothenic acid is widespread in foods. The AI (adequate intake) of pantothenic acid for adults is 5 mg/day. [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Niacin -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Niacin (a combination of nicotinic acid and nicotinamide), a B vitamin (vitamin B3), is a pharmacotherapeutic agent used since 1955, making it the oldest pleiotropic hypolipidemic agent. [1] The vitamin plays a role in neuroprotection and neuronal death, giving it the utmost importance in properly functioning the central nervous system, neuronal development, and function. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
agent1_snippets.content: Individuals with a pantothenate kinase 2 (PANK2) gene mutation are also likely to have a pantothenic acid inadequacy. PANK2 mutations can reduce the activity of pantothenate kinase 2, potentially decreasing the conversion of pantothenic acid to coenzyme A (CoA) and reducing CoA levels. PANK2 gene mutations also cause pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN). A common hallmark of individuals with PKAN is an iron accumulation in the brain, forming a pattern called the "eye of the tiger" sign. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Vitamins play a vital role in many biochemical functions in the human body and are essential components for maintaining optimal health. There are two main groups of vitamins – fat-soluble (easily stored in fat upon absorption) and water-soluble (washed out and not easily stored). Although adequate intake of all vitamins is important, regular intake is required to avoid deficiency due to the transient nature of water-soluble vitamins. The water-soluble vitamins include Vitamin C and Vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folate, and cobalamin).

agent1_snippets.title: Niacin Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: The basic principle of treating niacin deficiency is the correction of underlying causes of niacin deficiency, but replacement of niacin is a must. For susceptible persons, preventive measures reduce the risk of developing the disease. One of these steps is supplementation.

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B3 -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: The interprofessional team, including clinicians and pharmacists, should not empirically encourage patients to take niacin; it is only advised if there is a deficiency. When used for hyperlipidemia management, the vitamin often causes intolerable side effects, and the drug's use is declining. Most of the public should be encouraged to eat fresh fruit and vegetables to obtain their niacin. Reliance on supplements is not recommended because of adulterated and counterfeit products. [43]

agent1_snippets.content: Dietary supplements of vitamin B5 are available as vitamin B5 exclusively or in combination with other vitamins in multivitamin/multimineral products. Dexpanthenol administration can be administered via an oral spray in the mouth. [9]

agent2_snippets.content: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a constituent of the B‑vitamin family, which classifies it as a member of the broader B Vitamin Family. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1260
agent2_snippets.content: Calcium pantothenate (pantothenát vápenatý) is a calcium salt of pantothenic acid that belongs to the vitamin B complex and is classified chemically among calcium compounds. Its primary pharmacological action is phosphate‑chelating activity, which contributes to metabolic processes that require vitamin B5. Calcium pantothenate is a common active ingredient in a wide range of oral multivitamin and iron preparations, including products such as “ASCORBIC ACID 25 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 2 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACIN 1.8 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12.5 µg / MAGNESIUM SULFATE 5 mg / ZINC SULFATE 10 mg / CUPRIC SULFATE 2 mg / DOCONEXENT 156 mg / ICOSAPENT 39 mg (Dothelle DHA)”, “BETA CAROTENE 600 µg / Vitamin A Palmitate 600 µg / Ascorbic Acid 60 mg / Cholecalciferol 10 µg / α‑Tocopherol 20.1 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.7 mg / Riboflavin 2 mg / Niacinamide 20 mg / Pyridoxine Hydrochloride 2.5 mg / Folic Acid 1000 µg / Cyanocobalamin 8 µg /

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a core member of the vitamin B complex and is routinely incorporated as an active ingredient in a wide range of oral multivitamin‑mineral supplements, from diabetic‑specific formulations to prenatal and general‑health products. Typical niacin doses span from low nutritional levels (~1.8 mg) to therapeutic amounts (up to 40 mg) used for lipid management in diabetic patients, and the vitamin may also appear as an inactive excipient in certain preparations. Within the B‑vitamin spectrum, niacin is biochemically linked to pantothenic acid (vitamin B5), which together support energy metabolism and co‑enzyme functions. Pantothenic acid’s calcium salt, calcium pantothenate (pantothenát vápenatý), is the biologically active form of vitamin B5 and belongs to the same vitamin B complex. Calcium pantothenate contributes to coenzyme A synthesis, cellular activity alteration, and phosphate‑chelating activity, and it is a standard component of many multivitamin and mineral

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
agent2_snippets.content: The community organizes “vitaminy” as a central classification that unites individual vitamins (α‑tocopherol/α‑tocoferol, cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃), calcitriol, vitamin K, vitamin K₂, vitamin K₃, vitamin C/ascorbic acid, vitamin A, vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂, folic acid, β‑carotene, thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxocobalamin, hydroxokobalamin, retinoids, cholin) and their complexes, precursors, and analogues (e.g., dihydrotachysterol, retinoidy). These entities are hierarchically linked to broader groups such as fat‑soluble vitamins (parent of vitamin K and retinoids), steroid hormones (including the active vitamin D metabolite calcitriol and cholecalciferol), and the “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” category. Multivitamin agents, multivitamin preparations, and multivitamin + mineral preparations are positioned within the vitaminy framework, exemplified by the prenatal multivitamin product (VITAMIN A acetate

Inverse‑ISA statements integrate combined entities such as “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two” and “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂” into the vitaminy class. Minerals are closely linked to vitamins; multivitamins that contain minerals are considered vitamins and are associated with the mineral group, which is a subset of selenium—a trace element that connects to broader heavy‑metal categories. Nutritional deficiency (نقص التغذية) is associated with both vitamins and minerals, emphasizing the clinical importance of adequate intake.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1379
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is classified as a member of the vitamin B complex and is a common active ingredient in a wide variety of multivitamin and iron‑containing supplements. These products, which are available in capsule, tablet, liquid‑filled and powder forms, frequently combine niacin with other B‑vitamins such as thiamine (vitamin B1), riboflavin (vitamin B2), pyridoxine (vitamin B6), cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), biotin, and calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5). In addition, the formulations often include vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), folic acid, and a range of essential minerals including iron (ferrous fumarate or ferrous aspartate), zinc, copper, magnesium, manganese, and calcium. The niacin content varies widely among products, from low doses of about 1.8 mg to high‑potency levels of up to 800 mg, reflecting its use both for general nutritional support and for therapeutic purposes. Some supplement labels also note a relationship between pantothenic acid (kyselina pantothenová) and niacin, underscoring the interconnected role of B‑vitamins in metabolic health. Together, these combined formulations aim to address multiple micronutrient deficiencies, support energy metabolism, and promote overall patient well‑being.
